abstract:
Abstract
                  
                    The deployment of autonomous vehicles (AVs) and the accompanying societal and economic benefits will greatly depend on how much liability AV firms will have to carry for accidents involving these vehicles, which in turn impacts their insurability and associated insurance premiums. Across three experiments (
                    N
                     = 2677), we investigate whether accidents where the AV was not at fault could become an unexpected liability risk for AV firms, by exploring consumer perceptions of AV liability. We find that when such accidents occur, the not‐at‐fault vehicle becomes more salient to consumers when it is an AV. As a result, consumers are more likely to view as relevant counterfactuals in which the not‐at‐fault vehicle might have behaved differently to avoid or minimize damage from, the accident. This leads them to judge AV firms as more liable than both firms that make human‐driven vehicles and human drivers for damages when not at fault.

ob.ai_reason: 涉及自动驾驶车辆但非AI核心问题
ob.tldr: 通过三项实验（N=2677）考察消费者对自动驾驶车辆事故责任感知。结果显示，即使事故由人类司机所致，AV仍更突出于消费者判断，使其认为AV企业应承担更大赔偿责任，导致相对人类驾驶情境的责任归因提升。

title: Public perception and autonomous vehicle liability
title_zh: 公众认知与自动驾驶责任
